title: When is the Geodesic Flow Ergodic?
speaker: Dragomir Saric
abstract: The geodesic flow (for the hyperbolic metric) of an infinite Riemann surface is ergodic if and only if Brownian motion is recurrent, which is also equivalent to the divergence of the Poincaré series. Surfaces with ergodic geodesic flows are most similar to compact surfaces. We give various sufficient conditions on the Fenchel-Nielsen parameters that guarantee a surface falls into this class. Notably, we prove a version of the conjecture of J. Kahn and V. Markovic, which states that any surface with arbitrarily large cuff lengths and one topological end will have an ergodic geodesic flow when the twists are appropriately chosen. These findings are the result of several collaborative projects with Ara Basmajian, Hrant Hakobyan, and Michael Pandazis.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Hopf-Tsuji-Sullivan modulus criterion
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up an infinite hyperbolic/Riemann surface X=H/Gamma and identifies ergodicity of the geodesic flow with the parabolic/null-Green condition, equivalently the vanishing modulus of curves connecting a compact core to the ideal boundary.

proof_stages.label: Fenchel-Nielsen formulation of the question
proof_stages.summary: After choosing a pants decomposition, the surface is encoded by cuff lengths and normalized twists, and the main problem becomes to read the null-Green/ergodicity condition directly from these Fenchel-Nielsen parameters for flute, Loch Ness, and countable-end surfaces.

proof_stages.label: Standard collar extremal-distance bound
proof_stages.summary: Using disjoint hyperbolic collars around the cuff geodesics, every curve from the compact core to infinity is decomposed into collar-crossing subcurves, giving a summed extremal-length lower bound and the first sufficient condition, roughly sum l_n^{-1}e^{-l_n/2}=infinity.

proof_stages.label: Zero-twist completeness gap
proof_stages.summary: For zero twists the orthogeodesic segments line up, so metric completeness/first-kindness is governed by the larger divergence condition sum e^{-l_n/2}=infinity, exposing a gap in the standard-collar ergodicity criterion.

proof_stages.label: Nonstandard collars and zero-twist theorem
proof_stages.summary: The proof enlarges each standard collar by the thin slivers available inside a pair of pants and estimates the resulting modulus via logarithmic coordinates and height integrals, removing the extra 1/l_n factor and proving that a zero-twist flute is in the null-Green class exactly when it is complete.

proof_stages.label: Twist-sensitive nonstandard collar criterion
proof_stages.summary: Allowing nonzero twists changes how much of the enlarged collar can be used, leading to the sufficient condition sum exp[-(1/2)(1-|t_n|)l_n]=infinity for ergodicity and analogous countable-end/Loch Ness statements under bounded internal cutoff geometry.

proof_stages.label: Kahn-Markovic twisting conjecture and large-length theorem
proof_stages.summary: Motivated by the idea that suitable twists can kick geodesics back, the Kahn-Markovic conjecture asks for ergodic twists for arbitrary prescribed lengths, and the speaker states a new partial result: for any lower bounds a_n one can choose l_n>=a_n with all twists equal to 1/2 so that the flute has ergodic geodesic flow.

proof_stages.label: Half-twist sharpness and symmetry mechanism
proof_stages.summary: In the symmetric half-twist case a concavity assumption gives a sharp iff criterion sum e^{-l_n/4}=infinity, and the proof exploits front-back reflection to reduce the modulus problem to a simply connected half-surface whose end behaves like a point.

examples.name: Rectangle modulus computation
examples.note: A rectangle with curves joining top to bottom makes the definition of modulus concrete and shows the extremal metric in the simplest conformal quadrilateral case.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the modulus of curves crossing an a-by-b rectangle as a/b and identifies the extremal metric

passes.reason: The rectangle modulus example singles out the constant allowable metric that realizes the infimum.

examples.name: Annulus and the zero-radius point
examples.note: The annulus computation illustrates how logarithmic coordinates turn modulus into a rectangle calculation and why the family joining a compact set to a point has modulus zero, a fact later recalled in the half-twist proof sketch.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cuts an annulus, maps by log z to a rectangle, computes the modulus, and analyzes the r1=0 point case

passes.reason: The zero-radius annulus collapses the inner boundary to a point and gives the limiting zero-modulus case.
Letting the inner radius of the annulus be zero collapses one boundary component to a point, a limiting boundary case with modulus zero.
The round annulus is rotationally symmetric, and the zero-radius case collapses the inner boundary to a limiting point with zero modulus.

The round annulus is pushed to the collapsed zero-inner-radius limiting case where the inner boundary becomes a point and the modulus is zero.
The annulus with inner radius set to zero is a collapsed limiting case giving the boundary value modulus zero.
The round annulus is a symmetric model, and the zero-radius case collapses the inner boundary to a point with minimal modulus zero.
Letting the annulus inner radius be zero collapses a boundary circle to a point and gives the minimal modulus zero in a rotationally symmetric limit case.

The rotationally symmetric annulus is taken to the limiting case where the inner boundary collapses to a point.
the round annulus is taken to the limiting case r1=0, collapsing one boundary component to a point and giving the minimal modulus zero

examples.name: Surface with free boundary
examples.note: An audience question about taking an open surface with boundary illustrates why the talk excludes situations where geodesics can escape through a genuine boundary rather than through an end of a complete hyperbolic surface.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only notes that a surface with free boundary lets geodesics escape and so is not in the intended setting

passes.reason: The free-boundary surface is used as an obstruction because geodesics can escape and the flow is not in the intended ergodic setting.

examples.name: Logarithmic cuff-length tests
examples.note: The choices l_n=2 log n and, with half twists, l_n=4 log n demonstrate numerically how the improved twist-sensitive divergence condition is stronger than the earlier length-only one.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker plugs logarithmic cuff lengths such as ln=2 log n and ln=4 log n into the divergence tests and compares the resulting series

passes.reason: The logarithmic cuff-length choices sit at the harmonic-series divergence thresholds in the stated tests.

examples.name: Random twists
examples.note: In response to a question, random choices of twist parameters are used as a comparison to the existential Kahn-Markovic conjecture, with tentative completeness thresholds around n log n but no ergodicity theorem yet.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker discusses random twists and states known or conjectural threshold facts without deriving them or running the construction concretely

passes.reason: Fast-growth random twists are invoked to show that a random choice can be incomplete and hence cannot guarantee ergodicity.
Random twists are invoked to show that a naive random-choice version of the twisting conjecture can fail, since fast-growing lengths give almost surely incomplete surfaces.
Random twist choices are discussed as generic choices that for fast length growth are almost surely incomplete, ruling out a naive random-twist strategy.
random twists are discussed to show that random choices can fail to give completeness or ergodicity for sufficiently fast length growth
Random twists are discussed as showing that for sufficiently fast length growth random choices almost surely give incomplete, hence non-ergodic, surfaces.
Random twists are discussed as showing that a random-choice version can fail for sufficiently fast-growing lengths, since almost every surface is incomplete.
Random twists are discussed as failing for sufficiently fast length growth, where almost every resulting surface is incomplete and hence not ergodic.
Random twists are discussed to show that generic twisting can fail to give completeness or ergodicity for sufficiently fast length growth.
random twists are discussed as typical choices that for sufficiently large lengths are almost surely incomplete, so they defeat the naive random-twist heuristic
Random twists are invoked to refute the heuristic that a random choice should make the surface complete or ergodic for very fast cuff growth.
Random twists are used to show that random choices can almost surely give incomplete surfaces for fast length growth, so they do not generally force ergodicity.
